diagrams.description: This timeline traces the intellectual and practical development of Urban Studies. It begins with early urban sociology: Simmel’s ‘The Metropolis and Mental Life’ (1903) and the Chicago School’s urban ecology (1920s). Mid‑20th century saw Jane Jacobs’s critique of modernist planning in ‘The Death and Life of Great American Cities’ (1961). Henri Lefebvre’s ‘Le Droit à la Ville’ (1968) introduced the right to the city. David Harvey’s ‘Social Justice and the City’ (1973) grounded critical urban theory. The 1980s‑90s brought the world city hypothesis (Friedmann, Sassen), postmodern urbanism, and the Los Angeles School. The 2000s‑2010s saw the rise of the creative city (Florida), assemblages, planetary urbanization (Brenner), and the smartphone‑enabled smart city. Contemporary urban studies emphasize pandemic urbanism (2020), climate resilience, and decolonial urbanism.
